Procurement Window Watch

Wholesale Energy Markets Softened on Diplomatic Hopes, but Physical Risk Remains Unchanged

Current pricing remains exposed to risk, but the forward market may still offer better value further out. Customers inside 12 months of renewal should review now, because waiting does not guarantee access to those future prices later. The market is cheaper further out — but that opportunity only exists while your contract end date allows you to buy that future delivery period.

Report: 26 May 2026 Research: 22–26 May 2026 Extended: 19–26 May 2026
Trading Economics MarketWatch World Bank EIA

Market Further Out: Is the market cheaper further out?

Yes. The forward curve is in backwardation — meaning energy for delivery further in the future is currently cheaper than near-term delivery. This is the market's way of saying today's prices carry more immediate risk.

Near-Term

Gas forward prices (p/therm) — illustrative based on settlement data. Source: Smart Energy / ICE

?
Futures Market Explained in Plain English

What the futures market is

The futures market is the market price for energy delivered in a future period. When the near-term market is expensive but the further-out market is cheaper, it usually means today's price contains more immediate risk — such as supply pressure, shipping uncertainty, weather risk, storage concern or geopolitical premium.

For a customer whose contract starts 12 months from now, the relevant question is not only what energy costs today. It is what the market is offering for their delivery period. If that future period is cheaper, the customer may be able to contract into that lower forward market now.

Why waiting can be expensive

Customers often assume that if the 12-month or 18-month market is cheaper today, they can wait and pick it up later. That is not how the market works. The future price is available because the customer is buying that future delivery period now. If the customer waits, the delivery period moves closer, current risk gets built into the price, and supplier books can change.

In simple terms, the cheaper forward market can become the expensive current market.

💡 Commercial Message for Customers

If your contract end date is far enough out, you may be able to contract into a market that is cheaper than the near-term market. That can support fixing longer where the price, contract date, usage profile and risk position make sense.

Cheaper further out does not mean cheaper later. The future market is only useful if you act while it is still future pricing. Waiting means the customer stays exposed while today's future market becomes tomorrow's current market.

If wholesale prices move before supplier books fully catch up, there may be a short window to secure pricing. That window can close quickly. If the forward market is cheaper than the near-term market, the customer should check whether their contract end date allows them to buy into that future period now.

⚠ Commercial Takeaway

Markets remain structurally volatile. Short-term pauses in geopolitical escalation have not normalised wholesale pricing conditions. Businesses approaching renewal windows should review procurement strategy early rather than waiting for stability.

The market may look calmer today, but unless physical supply, shipping and supplier prices have properly improved, waiting still keeps you exposed.

If the market is cheaper further out, that can be useful only while the customer is still able to buy that future delivery period. Waiting does not guarantee those cheaper future prices become available later.

Prices correct as of report date. Wholesale prices are indicative and subject to change. Past performance is not a reliable indicator of future results.

Risk Assessment

Top Five Warning Signs

Ranked commercial risks for UK business energy buyers this week

1
Hormuz Remains Effectively Closed Despite Diplomatic Headlines

Verified fact: The IRGC confirmed closure on 2 March. Tanker traffic is at ~5% of pre-war average per Kpler vessel data. 1,550+ vessels remain stranded. Carra Globe Hormuz Monitor

Why it matters: Selective passage for Chinese or approved vessels is not normalisation. Europe and UK still face constrained LNG and oil supply. This makes the World Bank baseline (assuming disruption ends in May) look increasingly optimistic.

2
Trump Delayed Attack — But Warning Stands

Verified: Trump announced on 18 May that he delayed a planned attack after requests from Qatar, Saudi Arabia and UAE leaders. He stated military remains ready for "full, large scale assault" if deal not reached. WWNY Wikipedia

Why it matters: Gulf-state intervention reduces immediate military risk but keeps the market in limbo. Trump has warned Tehran that "the clock is ticking." The delayed strike can return at short notice if talks fail.

3
Deal "Close" But Not Agreed — Market Reacting to Optimism

Reports only: As of 24 May, reports indicated US and Iran nearing a broader peace agreement. Trump stated a memorandum of understanding was "close to finalisation." Wikipedia

Why it matters: "Closer" is not the same as agreed, accepted or implemented. Markets are pricing in diplomatic optimism. If talks fail, prices can re-escalate rapidly. UK businesses should not treat headline relief as physical de-escalation.

4
Supplier Books May Not Have Fully Caught Up

Inferred from market behaviour: Many suppliers withdrew pricing during peak volatility in March-April. Most have returned but remain cautious. Quote validity has shortened. WeSave

Why it matters: If wholesale markets have moved before supplier books fully reprice, there may be a short procurement window. That window closes as soon as suppliers update their pricing models.

5
World Bank Baseline Already Prices in 24% Energy Price Rise

Verified: World Bank Commodity Markets Outlook (April 2026) projects energy prices to surge 24% in 2026, with Brent averaging $86/barrel. This assumes the most acute disruptions end in May. World Bank

Why it matters: Even if disruption eases, the World Bank baseline points to materially higher energy prices. Waiting is a bet that the World Bank, EIA and current market pricing are all overstating the risk.

Delta Analysis

What Changed Since the Last Report

Material new developments in the primary research window (22–26 May 2026)

↓
Market Conditions

Gas softened: NBP day-ahead fell from ~121p/th (21 May) to 117p/th (26 May) on diplomatic progress reports. TTF Jun-26 future at €48.16, down from €51+ mid-week. Trading Economics

Power followed gas: Day-ahead baseload volatile, ranging £84-116/MWh depending on wind output. Forward curves show Summer 2027 at £74.86/MWh — cheaper than near-term. Catalyst

Brent consolidated: Crude at $98.24/bbl, down from $116+ in early May but still 54% above year-ago levels. Market pricing in a partial, not full, resolution. Trading Economics

📣
Geopolitical & Diplomatic

Trump delayed attack (18 May): Gulf state leaders (Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE) requested delay. Trump complied but instructed military to remain ready for "full, large scale assault." WWNY

Deal reports (24 May): Trump stated MOU "close to finalisation." Proposed agreement includes temporary moratorium on Iranian enrichment and phased reopening of maritime routes. Not yet agreed. Wikipedia

Hormuz status unchanged: Restricted since 27 February. No verified full reopening. Chinese vessel passage reported but this is selective access, not normalisation. Hormuz Monitor

⚡
Forward Market & Procurement Context

Further-Out Pricing

Cal-27 gas at 94.73p/th vs Jun-26 at 120.71p — a 21% discount for delivery 12 months further out. Cal-28 at 71.15p, a 41% discount. Smart Energy

Supplier Behaviour

Suppliers who withdrew pricing in March-April have mostly returned. Books remain cautious. Fixed offers available but quote validity shortened. Rollover rates remain elevated. WeSave

Commercial Interpretation

Temporary softness from diplomatic headlines has created a potential procurement window. But physical supply, shipping and storage conditions have not materially improved. This is headline relief, not de-escalation.

Full Timeline: 22–26 May 2026

18 May 2026 — Morning
Trump announces delayed Iran attack on Truth Social
States he was "asked by leaders of Qatar, Saudi Arabia and UAE to hold off on planned military attack." Says "serious negotiations are now taking place." Instructs national security team to remain ready for "full, large scale assault" if no deal. ✓ Verified: Trump post + multiple outlets WWNY

Trump confirms to reporters: attack was "scheduled for tomorrow"
Says attack put off "for a little while, hopefully maybe forever, but possibly for a little while." Commercial relevance: immediate military risk reduced, but threat remains conditional. ✓ Verified: Reporter quotes WWNY
19 May 2026
UK gas holds near 121p/th; Brent at $105-107/bbl
Markets digest Trump delay. Wholesale gas off recent highs but forward curve remains elevated. Norwegian maintenance and Ichthys LNG strike threat (from 27 May) keep forward contracts above 122p. Catalyst
20 May 2026
Spot power falls 22% on warm weather and strong renewables
Day-ahead electricity drops sharply due to high wind and solar output. However, forward gas contracts hold above 122p as underlying supply risks persist. Demonstrates how UK power follows weather and gas simultaneously. Catalyst
21 May 2026
UK gas eases 2-3% on Iran diplomatic progress reports
Day-ahead NBP at ~121p/th. TTF falling toward €48. Brent softens to $99-100/bbl range. Market beginning to price in possibility of deal, but physical supply unchanged. Smart Energy
22 May 2026
NBP settles at 118.47p/th; TTF Jun-26 at €48.155
End-of-week settlement shows continued softening from peaks. Forward curve maintains backwardation — Cal-27 at 94.73p, Cal-28 at 71.15p. Market pricing in gradual normalisation but not immediate relief. Barchart MarketWatch
24 May 2026
Reports: US and Iran nearing broader peace agreement
Trump states memorandum of understanding "close to finalisation." Suggests Strait of Hormuz could reopen under ceasefire framework. Proposed deal includes temporary enrichment moratorium and phased maritime reopening. ⚠ Reports only — not yet agreed or implemented Wikipedia
25–26 May 2026
Markets consolidate at lower levels; NBP at 117p/th, Brent $97-98/bbl
Holiday-thinned trading. Prices holding at softening but still elevated levels. Year-on-year: NBP +32%, Brent +54%. Diplomatic optimism priced in; physical market conditions unchanged. Trading Economics Trading Economics

Rhetoric Tracker

What key actors said, and whether it matters commercially

T
Donald Trump
US President
"I have been asked by the leaders of Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates to hold off on our planned Military attack... serious negotiations are now taking place... a Deal will be made."

● Verified: Truth Social post, 18 May.
● Operationally meaningful: Yes — delayed military action, but maintained readiness threat.
● Market impact: Immediate softening in oil and gas. WWNY

G
Gulf State Leaders
Qatar, Saudi Arabia, UAE
Requested Trump delay attack to allow negotiations to continue. Reportedly believe a deal is possible and would be "acceptable to the United States."

● Verified via Trump statement: Confirmed by Trump as his reason for delay. Independent Gulf confirmation not separately verified.
● Operationally meaningful: Reduces immediate military risk but prolongs uncertainty.
● Market impact: Contributed to short-term softness. KCRA

I
Iranian Leadership
Government & negotiators
Reports indicate Iran sent amended proposal through Pakistani mediators. Position on enrichment remains a sticking point.

● Partially verified: Media reports of proposal, but Iranian official confirmation limited.
● Operationally meaningful: Only if deal is finalised and implemented.
● Market impact: Optimism priced in; failure would reverse gains. Wikipedia

W
World Bank
Commodity Markets Outlook, April 2026
"Energy prices are projected to surge by 24% this year... the war is hitting the global economy in cumulative waves: first through higher energy prices, then higher food prices, and finally higher inflation."

● Verified: Official press release, 28 April 2026.
● Operationally meaningful: Yes — baseline forecast guides institutional and commercial planning.
● Market impact: Validates elevated pricing outlook even if disruption eases. World Bank

Supply Chain Risk

Shipping and Choke Point Status

⛵
Strait of Hormuz
~5%
of pre-war traffic
Source: Kpler vessel data via Carra Globe
1,550+
vessels stranded
Source: Joint Chiefs, 6 May
22,500
mariners trapped
Source: Gen. Dan Caine
Closed
since 2 Mar 2026
IRGC confirmed closure

Selective access is not normalisation. Chinese vessel passage and approved vessel transit has been reported, but this does not mean Europe or the UK has normalised supply conditions. P&I insurance remains cancelled for most operators. War-risk premiums are elevated. Carra Globe Hormuz Monitor

🚢
Bab el-Mandeb / Red Sea
-49%
Suez transits vs pre-crisis
Carra Globe data
Cape Route
primary alternative
+10-14 days transit

Dual choke point crisis continues. Houthi attacks resumed 28 February. Most major carriers (Maersk, MSC, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd) rerouting via Cape of Good Hope. Suez Canal transits remain severely depressed. Container and LNG flows to Europe disrupted. Carra Globe

Impact on UK energy: LNG cargoes from Qatar — which supplied ~20% of UK gas pre-crisis — remain severely constrained. UK storage at 10%, lowest in Europe. This structural tightness keeps UK gas prices elevated even when headline geopolitical risk softens.

⚠ Commercial Point: Dual Choke Point Risk

The Strait of Hormuz and Bab el-Mandeb are two separate choke points, both currently disrupted. Even if Hormuz reopens partially, Bab el-Mandeb remains affected by Houthi activity. A dual crisis means there is no quick fix to global shipping routes. For UK businesses, this means sustained pressure on LNG availability, oil prices, and ultimately gas and electricity costs through 2026 and potentially into 2027.

Institutional Baselines

World Bank vs EIA

Comparing institutional forecasts and what they mean for UK business buyers

🌐
World Bank Baseline
+24%
energy price surge in 2026
Commodity Markets Outlook, April 2026
  • ● Brent forecast: $86/barrel average for 2026 (vs $69 in 2025)
  • ● Assumes most acute disruptions end in May 2026
  • ● Assumes Hormuz shipping gradually returns to pre-war levels by late 2026
  • ● Overall commodity prices: +16% in 2026
  • ● Fertilizer prices: +31% (urea +60%)
Analyst Interpretation

The World Bank baseline already points to materially higher energy prices even if disruption eases. If the acute phase does not end in May — and Hormuz remains restricted as of late May — then the baseline may be optimistic. The higher-risk scenario (not the baseline) would see even larger price increases.

World Bank Press Release CMO Report PDF
⚡
EIA Short-Term Outlook
$95+
Brent near-term, then $70 by year-end
STEO, March & May 2026
  • ● Brent remains above $95/b for next 2 months
  • ● Falls below $80/b in Q3 2026
  • ● Reaches ~$70/b by year-end; averages $64 in 2027
  • ● Assumes Hormuz closure causes further production shut-ins in coming weeks
  • ● Assumes gradual easing as transit resumes; highly dependent on conflict duration
Analyst Interpretation

The EIA is more bearish on oil prices than the World Bank for late 2026/2027, but its near-term forecast ($95+) is still elevated. The EIA explicitly states its forecast is "highly dependent" on conflict duration assumptions. If Hormuz does not reopen by late summer, the EIA's $70 year-end target may prove optimistic.

EIA STEO March EIA STEO May PDF

What This Comparison Means for UK Businesses

Both institutions agree on one thing: energy prices in 2026 will be materially higher than 2025, even under their baseline/optimistic scenarios. The World Bank says +24% for energy. The EIA says Brent at $95+ near-term before moderating.

The key commercial question is: do you believe the risk is being overstated? If the World Bank baseline (which already assumes disruption easing) points to a 24% rise, and current market conditions show Hormuz still restricted, supplier books cautious, and LNG supply constrained — then waiting is a bet that all of these institutions and market signals are wrong.

If the EIA's more bearish late-2026 view proves correct, customers who fixed earlier at elevated levels may appear to have overpaid. But if the World Bank's higher-risk scenario materialises — or if disruption persists beyond May — those who waited will face even higher prices with no protection.

Procurement Timing

Supplier Price-Book Lag and Procurement Window

📪
How Supplier Price Books Work

Supplier price books are the internal pricing models that energy suppliers use to quote fixed-rate contracts to business customers. These books are built on wholesale forward curves, supplier hedging costs, risk margins, and operational overheads.

When wholesale markets move quickly — up or down — supplier price books do not update instantly. There is a lag, typically ranging from hours to several days, depending on the supplier's systems, risk appetite, and trading desk capacity.

During the March-April 2026 volatility, many suppliers withdrew pricing entirely. As of late May, most have returned, but books remain cautious and quote validity periods have shortened from the typical 5-7 days to 24-48 hours in many cases. WeSave

⏰
The Procurement Window

If wholesale markets have moved down before supplier books fully catch up, there may be a short window to secure pricing at levels that do not yet reflect the full supplier repricing.

This window is inherently temporary. As soon as suppliers update their forward curve inputs, adjust risk premiums, and reload their pricing models, the opportunity closes. In volatile periods, this can happen within 24-48 hours.

For customers within 12 months of contract end date, the message is clear: do not wait for perfect clarity. Test pricing now. Compare short-term and longer-term options. If the forward market is cheaper further out, explore whether your contract end date allows you to buy into that future delivery period.

💡 Why This Matters for SMEs

Small and medium businesses often assume that supplier pricing tracks wholesale markets in real time. It does not. There is always a lag. In normal markets, this lag is negligible. In volatile markets, it creates genuine — but temporary — opportunities.

If you are a business owner, facilities manager, or procurement lead reading this: the time to act is before the supplier book catches up, not after. Get a quote. Compare your options. The worst-case scenario is that you have current market information to make an informed decision. The best-case is that you secure pricing before the next move.

Commercial Guidance

Customer Action: What UK Businesses Should Do Now

1
Within 12 Months of Renewal

Act now. Review your contract end date. If you are within 12 months, you should be actively testing supplier pricing. Do not wait for your renewal window to officially open.

The forward market may offer cheaper pricing for future delivery periods. If your contract end date aligns with a cheaper forward period, you may be able to lock in that pricing now.

Waiting is not neutral. It is a bet that the World Bank, EIA, current market pricing, and all visible supply risks are overstated. WeSave

2
Multi-Site & High-Consumption Users

Prioritise your largest meters. High-consumption electricity and gas users have the most exposure to wholesale price moves. Even small changes in unit rate translate to significant cost impacts at scale.

Consider structured procurement: fix baseload demand for 36+ months where forward pricing supports it, and leave flexible components open if your risk appetite allows.

Multi-site operators should review each site's contract end date independently — they rarely align. Connection Technologies

3
On Variable, Deemed or Rollover Rates

Highest urgency. If you are on a variable, deemed, rollover, or out-of-contract rate, you have zero protection against wholesale price spikes. Every day you remain exposed is a day the market can move against you.

These rates are typically the most expensive and most volatile. Fixing into a fixed-rate contract — even at current elevated levels — provides budget certainty and removes exposure to further upside.

If forward pricing is cheaper further out, consider fixing longer to capture that backwardation. Green Compare

The Core Commercial Message

Waiting is not neutral. It is a bet that the risk is being overstated.

For customers within 12 months of contract end date, the commercially sensible step is to review immediately, test available supplier pricing, compare short-term and longer-term pricing, and — where pricing and risk profile support it — consider fixing now and fixing longer (36+ months) before supplier books move further.

If the customer can currently contract into a cheaper future market, waiting does not protect that price. Waiting simply moves the customer closer to delivery, where today's risk premium can become tomorrow's contract price.

Cheaper further out does not mean cheaper later. It means there may be an opportunity now, while that period is still priced as future delivery. This is not automatic advice to fix. It is a reason to review now.

Source Quality and Confidence

Highest Confidence

Medium Confidence

  • ● Supplier book behaviour: Inferred from WeSave, WeSave market commentary — not direct supplier data
  • ● Gulf state requests to Trump: Via Trump statement — independent Gulf confirmation not separately verified
  • ● Deal "close" reports: Media reports 24 May — not confirmed by Iranian or US officials as implemented
  • ● Quote validity shortening: Inferred from normal market behaviour during volatility — not quantified by supplier data
?

  • ● Exact terms of any US-Iran MOU — no published text available
  • ● Whether Hormuz will reopen under any ceasefire framework
